mappings.rationale: Covers the history of visual arts including sculpture, directly relevant to the section's treatment of Western sculpture history (section C.1).
Addresses theoretical questions about sculpture as art including design, representation, and nonrepresentation — topics covered in sections A and B.
Covers modern sculpture including abstract and kinetic forms, relevant to the section's treatment of nonrepresentational sculpture (section B.2.b) and Western sculpture history.
Covers contemporary sculptural practices including environmental sculpture and installation, relevant to sections B.1 and C.1.
Renaissance sculpture is a major period in Western sculpture history, directly relevant to section C.1.
Ancient Egyptian sculpture including devotional images, portraits, and commemorative monuments is relevant to the section's treatment of representational sculpture and its history.
Dada and Surrealist sculptors challenged traditional sculptural forms, relevant to the section's treatment of Western sculpture history and nonrepresentational sculpture.
Avant-garde sculpture expanded the definition and materials of sculpture, relevant to the section's treatment of kinetic, environmental, and nonrepresentational sculpture.
Christian sculpture including devotional images and commemorative monuments is directly relevant to sections B.2.a, B.3.a, and B.3.c.
Classical Greek and Roman sculpture is foundational to Western sculpture history and covers human figures, portraits, and devotional images — topics in section B.2.a.
Mythological subjects were central to classical sculpture, relevant to the section's treatment of representational sculpture and its iconography (section A.2).
Covers properties of materials including stone, metal, clay, and glass — sculptural materials explicitly listed in section A.3.
Covers prehistoric sculptural objects and figurines, relevant to the section's treatment of primitive and folk sculpture (section B.4).
Futurist sculpture explored kinetic forms and new materials, relevant to the section's treatment of kinetic sculpture and Western sculpture history.
Relevant to the section's treatment of primitive and folk sculpture (section B.4) and ceremonial objects and masks (sections B.3.a and B.3.d).
Archaeological methods are essential for studying ancient and primitive sculpture, relevant to the section's historical treatment of sculpture.
Etruscan sculpture including bronze figures and tomb art is a significant tradition in Western sculpture history.
Relevant to the section's treatment of elements of design and principles of composition in sculpture (section A.1).
